senses.definition: to be brave and responsible enough to accept all the consequences of one's or others' unpleasant deeds

examples.x: Even though it wasn't entirely his fault, he had to carry the can for the project's failure.
In the end, the manager had to carry the can for the team's poor performance.
The scapegoat often ends up carrying the can for the mistakes made by the entire organization.
She took it upon herself to carry the can for the misunderstanding and apologized to her friend.
The government officials are trying to avoid carrying the can for the controversial decision, shifting the blame to others.

senses.definition: to face the consequences of one's behavior or actions

examples.x: If you want quality work, you have to pay the piper for the expertise.
The company had to pay the piper for its unethical business practices when it faced legal consequences.
You can't enjoy all the benefits without paying the piper at some point.
Ignoring maintenance will eventually lead to costly repairs—someone has to pay the piper.
In the end, he had to pay the piper for his procrastination, facing the stress of last-minute work.

senses.definition: to have no option but to suffer or deal with the unfavorable effects of something, particularly one's deeds

examples.x: I sure paid the penalty for staying up late when I fell asleep at my desk in the library.
If you get caught cheating on your exam, you'll have to pay the penalty—which could include expulsion.
One day you will all pay the price for your selfish behaviour.
With data filtering one pays the price of decreasing the effective library redundancy and increasing the number of hybridisations.
After violating the rules, he had to pay the price with a suspension from the team.

senses.definition: the main and worst set of problems that are caused by someone or something

examples.x: He claimed that the middle class would bear the brunt of the tax increase.
Whatever meager rations remained were sent to the troops fighting the war, so the civilian population faced the brunt of the famine.
During the storm, the coastal towns bore the brunt of the damage, with severe flooding and destruction.
The youngest child in the family frequently ends up bearing the brunt of their siblings' teasing and pranks.
The small business felt the brunt of the new regulations, struggling to adapt and remain competitive.

senses.definition: to suffer the consequences of a business deal or a relationship gone wrong

examples.x: He invested heavily in that risky business venture and ended up burning his fingers when it failed.
She tried to meddle in their personal affairs and burned her fingers when they turned against her.
Getting involved in that scandalous gossip will only make you burn your fingers in the long run.
He borrowed money from the wrong people and quickly realized he had burned his fingers by getting entangled with loan sharks.
The politician's corruption eventually caught up with him, and he burned his fingers when the scandal was exposed."

senses.definition: to accept the consequences of the things one has done

examples.x: After a week of indulgence on vacation, we returned home to pay the fiddler with a pile of unpaid bills.
They threw a lavish party but had to pay the fiddler by dealing with the mess left behind.
Ignoring the maintenance of their car, they eventually had to pay the fiddler with costly repairs.
Enjoying a night out with friends is great, but you'll have to pay the fiddler by dealing with a late start the next morning.
Some people live for the moment, not realizing they'll have to pay the fiddler for their impulsive choices.

senses.definition: a very bad situation that is a result of the simultaneous occurrence of a series of unpleasant things

examples.x: The financial crisis of 2008 was a perfect storm of risky lending, housing market collapse, and global economic instability.
The project's failure was a result of a perfect storm of mismanagement, inadequate resources, and unforeseen challenges.
Her illness was caused by a perfect storm of stress, lack of sleep, and a weakened immune system.
The company faced a perfect storm of problems, including a data breach, public relations disaster, and legal challenges.
The traffic jam was a perfect storm of rush hour congestion, road closures, and bad weather.

senses.definition: a situation where something increases or gives rise to other things

examples.x: The positive feedback from early customers created a snowball effect, attracting more clients to the business.
A small disagreement among team members can escalate into a major conflict, thanks to the snowball effect.
A single lie can lead to a snowball effect of further lies as a person tries to cover their tracks.
Once the rumors started, they quickly spread through the community like a snowball effect.
The initial budget overrun triggered a snowball effect of financial issues that the project couldn't recover from.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ tə θˈæŋk fɔːɹ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to assume someone responsible for something good that happened to one and be grateful to them

examples.x: I have my parents to thank for my success; they always encouraged me to pursue my dreams.
She has her dedicated team to thank for the project's timely completion.

senses.definition: the time when one faces the consequences of one's past deeds or mistakes

examples.x: After years of embezzling money, he knew the day of reckoning would eventually arrive.
The company's unethical practices finally caught up with them, and the day of reckoning came in the form of a massive lawsuit.
She had been avoiding her responsibilities for too long, and the day of reckoning had arrived in the form of a failing grade.
The corrupt politician feared the day of reckoning when the truth about his actions would be revealed to the public.
Living in denial only postpones the day of reckoning when you must confront your problems.

senses.definition: to become existent or be born

examples.x: The doctors didn't think I would ever see the light of day because of my medical condition, so they were surprised when I was born a healthy 8 pounds, 10 ounces.
This software first saw the light of day back in 1993.
With the signing of the treaty, a powerful new European alliance first saw the light of day.
After years of research and development, the groundbreaking technology finally saw the light.
The idea for the new product first saw the light during a brainstorming session.

senses.definition: to experience significant and often negative results or expenses because of one's choices or actions

examples.x: After buying that expensive car, he paid dearly for it when he struggled to make the monthly payments.
Neglecting regular maintenance led to her paying dearly for the costly repair bills on her home.
Choosing to stay up late to study caused her to pay dearly the next day with exhaustion.
The company's failure to meet quality standards resulted in paying dearly through fines and customer loss.
His unhealthy lifestyle choices caught up with him, and he paid dearly with health problems later in life.

senses.definition: to suffer the unpleasant consequences of one's own deeds

examples.x: After spreading false rumors about her colleague, she had to stew in her own juice when the truth came out, and she lost the trust of her coworkers.
Some people have a habit of blaming others for their mistakes rather than stewing in their own juice and taking responsibility.
He decided to invest in the stock market without proper research and had to stew in his own juice when he lost a significant amount of money.
After cheating on the test, he had to stew in his own juices, facing both academic consequences and strained relationships with friends.

word_list.pronunciation: sˈoʊ plˈænt ðə sˈiːdz ʌv ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to do something that leads to the occurrence of something in the future, particularly something unpleasant

examples.x: By trusting Anna again, he's sowing the seeds of his own downfall.
They have planted the seeds of their own destruction.
The dictator's oppressive regime has sown the seeds of resistance and rebellion among the oppressed citizens.
Ignoring climate change warnings could plant the seeds of ecological catastrophe for future generations.
